Based on the evidence gathered, I would concur with my earlier assessment. The key reasons supporting why endodontically treated posterior teeth become more prone to fracturing than untreated counterparts stem primarily from hollow canals created post-procedure alongside reduced protective corona coverage. Hollow canals diminish inherent supportive structures internally while lost or inadequate coronal sealant externally leave teeth structurally vulnerable even against relatively minor stresses encountered normally via biting down forcefully on food items requiring strong jaw muscle action. Therefore, option **D** "All of the Above" appears accurate encapsulating all relevant contributory factors identified - loss of moisture, empty spaces within pulp-chambers, decreased coronal height/strength rendering teeth fragile and susceptible to failure under typical usage loads unlike similarly shaped untreated specimens maintaining robustness naturally endowed by nature pre-surgical alteration.
